name: urtica dioica
mechanism: Evidence suggests anti-inflammatory activity and possible effects on cytokine signaling; clinical evidence remains mixed by indication.
effects: adjunct symptom support in some inflammatory conditions
nutritive tonic use in traditional practice
No direct effects data. Contextual inference:
nan.. No direct mechanismofaction data. Contextual inference:
contraindications: use caution with antihypertensives and diuretics
safetyNotes: GI complaints (e.g., nausea/heartburn/flatulence/diarrhea) and allergic reactions (pruritus/rash/urticaria) possible; seek medical evaluation if red-flag urinary symptoms occur (e.g., blood in urine, urinary retention, painful urination, fever).

sources.title: European Union herbal monograph on *Urtica dioica* L.; *Urtica urens* L., radix (Final – Revision 1)
Urticae radix (Nettle root) – EMA inventory page

dosage: Adults/elderly men examples from EMA monograph include: herbal infusion 2 g in 150 mL water, 2–3 times daily; multiple extract-specific single daily dose (SD) / daily dose (DD) regimens are also listed and vary by preparation (DER/solvent).

region: Native range reported for Urtica dioica includes entity["place","Europe","continent"] to Siberia and W. China, plus NW. Africa; Urtica urens native range includes Europe to Siberia and Himalaya and parts of tropical Africa to the Arabian Peninsula.
preparation: Oral use as infusion ("herbal tea") or standardized extracts in solid/liquid dosage forms.
description: Herbal substance/preparations include comminuted root and multiple dry/liquid extracts (various DERs and ethanol percentages) intended for oral use.
sideEffects: contact urticaria from fresh plant hairs
mild gastrointestinal upset in some users
summary: Traditional herbal medicinal product for relief of lower urinary tract symptoms related to benign prostatic hyperplasia (BPH) after serious conditions have been excluded by a clinician (traditional use basis).
